channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: ‘City of Glass’ Is Under Threat From Iran War
item.description: In India, Firozabad has depended on its glassmaking industry for centuries. Fuel prices are starting to shatter businesses already under pressure.
item.dc:creator: Alex Travelli, Suhasini Raj and Atul Loke

item.title: Chizi, Standup Comic Exiled in China, Wants to Be More Than Just ‘a Rebel Comedian’
item.description: Having gotten into trouble for making jokes critical of the Chinese government, the standup comic Chizi now lives in self-imposed exile. He’s finding that freedom imposes its own constraints.
item.dc:creator: Li Yuan and Chang W. Lee
item.media:description: Chizi performed for the first time in years in Tokyo in April. The audience went to see a rebel.

item.title: Tractor-Trailer and Light Post Damaged as Plane Lands at Newark Airport
item.description: The tractor-trailer was on the New Jersey Turnpike on Sunday when it was damaged, the authorities said. Its driver had minor injuries and no one aboard the plane was hurt.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra E. Petri

item.title: Oil Prices Jump While Stock Futures Inch Up
item.description: Oil prices fell and futures on the S&P 500 rose slightly on Sunday as investors reacted to continued uncertainty about the war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Charles Krupa/Associated Press

item.title: 3 Dead in Hantavirus Outbreak Aboard Cruise Ship, W.H.O. Says
item.description: One case of hantavirus infection has been confirmed in a laboratory, the organization said on Sunday. There are five additional suspected cases.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra E. Petri and Livia Albeck-Ripka
item.media:credit: Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The cruise ship MV Hondius off Cape Verde on Sunday. Officials identified the ship as the one with passengers infected by hantavirus.

item.title: E.E.O.C. Investigating Discrimination Claim at The New York Times
item.description: The Equal Employment Opportunity Commission recently referred a reverse-discrimination complaint by an employee to the agency’s legal unit for review.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Davis O’Brien
item.media:credit: Lucia Vazquez for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Equal Employment Opportunity Commission is investigating a reverse-discrimination claim filed against The New York Times by a white male employee.

item.title: ‘Devil Wears Prada’ Sequel Leads Box Office With $77 Million
item.description: It was the biggest opening weekend for a traditional comedy in 11 years. One analyst called the debut “sensational.”
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes

item.title: Spirit Airlines’ Demise Could Help Other Airlines
item.description: Even in its reduced state, the company played an important role in forcing other airlines to keep fares low, some experts said.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi
item.media:credit: Tom Brenner for The New York Times
item.media:description: Spirit Airlines’ presence at an airport usually helped to push down the fares of all airlines operating there.

item.title: OPEC Plus, in Symbolic Gesture, to Increase Oil Production
item.description: The announcement came days after the United Arab Emirates withdrew from the group. The higher output will have little effect on global supplies.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries
item.media:credit: Christian Bruna/Getty Images
item.media:description: The OPEC headquarters in Vienna. Seven of the members of the consortium of oil producing nations on Sunday noted “the importance of adopting a cautious approach.”

item.title: Jeeves and Ask.com Shut Down After Almost 30 Years
item.description: The pioneering search engine shut down on May 1, after nearly 30 years in operation.
item.dc:creator: Ali Watkins
item.media:credit: Newscast/Universal Images Group, via Getty Images
item.media:description: The Ask.com search engine website, with the illustration of the character Jeeves.

item.title: MAHA Awaits Robert F. Kennedy Jr.’s Definition of Ultraprocessed Foods
item.description: Some yogurts and peanut butters could be considered ultraprocessed as scientists and MAHA supporters push for a strict definition.
item.dc:creator: Julie Creswell and Alice Callahan
item.media:credit: Sandy Carson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Companies are already seeing demand for many of their products soften as some consumers cut back on spending.

item.title: Driving Electric in Costa Rica Is Surprisingly Doable
item.description: The charging network is spotty, but it’s a small country.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.media:credit: Tony Cenicola/The New York Times
item.media:description: Costa Rica requires utilities to install fast-charging stations every 50 miles on national highways. But many chargers don’t work.

item.title: These Countries Embrace E.V.s to Avoid Oil Price Shocks
item.description: People in Costa Rica and other Latin American, Asian and African countries are increasingly buying electric vehicles to avoid spiking fuel prices.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing and Tony Cenicola
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Guangzhou Xiaopeng Motors Technology Co Ltd (Xpeng Motors)
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:credit: Tony Cenicola/The New York Times

item.title: Spirit Airlines Cancels All Flights, Stranding Passengers
item.description: The budget carrier abruptly canceled flights early on Saturday, leaving passengers to rush to make other plans. “Even if they go back into business, never again,” one traveler said.
item.dc:creator: Rylee Kirk
item.media:credit: Joe Raedle/Getty Images
item.media:description: Some passengers were in the middle of their trips when they learned that their Spirit Airlines flights had been canceled.

item.title: The Cannabis Industry’s New Best Friend? President Trump
item.description: The administration’s decision to relax federal regulations on medical marijuana comes with big tax breaks for many cannabis companies, and could drive new investment in the budding sector.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Southall
item.media:credit: Liam Kennedy for The New York Times
item.media:description: Products at the LaFayette, N.Y., facility of Beak & Skiff, a 115-year-old apple orchard that makes a line of cannabis and hemp products called Ayrloom.

item.title: Is Tooth Brushing at Work Gross?
item.description: Plus: Is it insane to refuse a raise?
item.dc:creator: Max Read
item.media:credit: Photo illustration by Margeaux Walter for The New York Times

item.title: 5 Money Lessons From People Caring for Their Elderly Parents
item.description: Generation X-ers and others shared stories of family crises, and we asked experts how to think about living longer than you expect.
item.dc:creator: Elizabeth Harris
item.media:credit: Dan Koeck for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sarah Coomber and her father, James Coomber, 84, at his retirement community in Fargo, N.D. Seeing her parents struggle prompted conversations with her husband about what the two of them want, Ms. Coomber said.

item.title: Spirit Airlines Shuts Down After Years of Struggle
item.description: Spirit once upended the industry by offering very low fares but was in its second bankruptcy in two years after years of struggle.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi
item.media:credit: Sarah Rice/Getty Images
item.media:description: The Spirit Airlines ticket counter at Detroit Metropolitan Wayne County Airport in Romulus, Mich., was empty after the airline shut down overnight on Saturday.

item.title: Not Even Japanese Bathhouses Are Immune From Shocks of Iran War
item.description: High fuel costs are imperiling the Japanese sento, a long-declining industry that remains a vital social lifeline for the country’s isolated elderly.
item.dc:creator: River Akira Davis, Kiuko Notoya and Kentaro Takahashi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Netflix Plans First Wide Theatrical Release With ‘Narnia’
item.description: The company is breaking from its longstanding policy of releasing movies exclusively on its streaming service.
item.dc:creator: Nicole Sperling
item.media:credit: Nina Westervelt for The New York Times
item.media:description: Greta Gerwig, the director of “Narnia: The Magician’s Nephew,” at the Academy Awards in 2024. Netflix said it would give “Narnia” an exclusive theatrical release starting Feb. 2.

item.title: Thermos Recalls 8.2 Million Jars and Bottles After Customers Are Injured
item.description: The company received reports that the stoppers on some of its products had “forcefully ejected,” striking customers and causing permanent vision loss in a few cases, safety regulators said.
item.dc:creator: John S.W. MacDonald

item.title: Charlie Kirk’s Former Security Chief Sues Candace Owens for Defamation
item.description: The lawsuit focuses on remarks made by Ms. Owens, a right-wing podcaster, about Mr. Kirk’s killing, highlighting a growing tension among factions of the American right.
item.dc:creator: Ken Bensinger
item.media:credit: Rebecca Noble for The New York Times
item.media:description: Candace Owens at AmericaFest, an event hosted by Turning Point USA, in Phoenix in 2022.

item.title: Spirit Airlines Prepares to Shut Down After Trump Administration Bailout Falls Through
item.description: The low-fare airline, which has struggled for years, had been trying to secure a $500 million lifeline from the Trump administration.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Hirsch and Niraj Chokshi
item.media:credit: Wilfredo Lee/Associated Press
item.media:description: Spirit Airlines will stop flying after failing to find a way to emerge from bankruptcy in a time of high fuel prices and stiff competition.

item.title: Schwab Affiliate Halts Customer Donations to Southern Poverty Law Center
item.description: Fidelity’s and Vanguard’s donor-advised fund entities have taken similar actions since a Justice Department indictment of the civil rights group.
item.dc:creator: Ron Lieber
item.media:credit: Charles Krupa/Associated Press
item.media:description: Donor-advised funds let people create accounts, put donations in them, and take a tax deduction for the full amount that year. Then, they can parcel out donations to charities over time.

item.title: Trump Administration Pushes Forward With Tariffs Based on Forced Labor Laws
item.description: Nonprofit groups have applauded an investigation on trade in goods made with forced labor but say that the Trump administration should go further to enforce its own laws
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.media:credit: Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Cotton picked in the Chinese region of Xinjiang at a rail station in Jiujiang, China. A U.S. law bans most products from Xinjiang, but some have called on the United States to do more to enforce it.

item.title: Oil Cools Slightly After a 4-Year High as Gasoline Jumps
item.description: The price of Brent crude, the international benchmark, dipped, while the average price of gasoline in the United States bumped up by 9 cents overnight. Stocks continued to climb.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: F.D.A. Grants Early Access to Promising Drug for Pancreatic Cancer
item.description: Patients with one of the deadliest cancers have been pleading for an unapproved treatment that may prolong their lives.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Robbins and Gina Kolata
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: “Every month that that drug is delayed means that I have to get chemo two more times,” said Dr. Ronald Silvestri, 77, a retired pulmonologist who has metastatic pancreatic cancer.

item.title: Maryland Is First to Ban A.I.-Driven Price Increases in Grocery Stores
item.description: A state law that goes into effect in October prohibits grocery stores and third-party delivery services from using consumer data to boost prices.
item.dc:creator: John S.W. MacDonald
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Maryland bill comes amid increasing pushback against so-called dynamic pricing in states across America.

item.title: Stocks and Oil Prices Sent Conflicting Signals in April Amid Havoc of Iran War
item.description: The S&P 500 notched five weeks of gains for the first time in roughly 18 months. In April, it rose more than 10 percent, its best month since November 2020.
item.dc:creator: Joe Rennison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Trump’s Tariff Reversal Reopens a Major Market for Scotch Whisky
item.description: A 10 percent tax on American imports was hurting British sales, even though exporters had increased sales in India, China and other countries.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
item.media:credit: Emily Whitney for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Scottish whisky industry said it had been losing millions of pounds a week because of the American import tax.

item.title: Utility Bills Are Likely to Be Higher This Summer. Here’s What You Can Do.
item.description: The average bill is forecast to rise 8.5 percent. There are simple ways to make sure you’re not overpaying, like getting your air-conditioning checked.
item.dc:creator: Ann Carrns
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)

item.title: OpenAI’s New Model Spurs Debate Over Computing Power
item.description: Sam Altman suggested it would be released more widely than a rival offering from Anthropic. Some are suggesting it’s because OpenAI has more computing power.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.media:credit: Brennan Smart for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sam Altman, right, has suggested that OpenAI, when releasing its latest A.I. model, would take a different approach than its rival, Anthropic, did.

item.title: Fed Officials Cite Inflation Concerns in Defending Dissents
item.description: The Federal Reserve’s meeting this week was the most divisive in decades, a sign that President Trump’s pick to lead the central bank will face opposition if he pursues substantially lower interest rates.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jerome Powell speaking at the Federal Reserve on Wednesday.

item.title: Why U.S. Oil Companies Are Not Plugging the World’s Energy Gap
item.description: American producers are under pressure from investors to keep spending in check, and they are wary of drilling more wells because they are not sure oil prices will stay high.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: J. Emilio Flores for The New York Times
item.media:description: An oil field near Bakersfield, Calif. Western oil companies are reaping the rewards of higher energy prices, but are cautious about digging new wells.

item.title: Reporters at McClatchy Withhold Bylines in A.I. Dispute
item.description: Journalists at newspapers like The Miami Herald and The Sacramento Bee are refusing to let the chain use their names on summarized articles generated by a new A.I. tool.
item.dc:creator: Katie Robertson
item.media:credit: Wilfredo Lee/Associated Press
item.media:description: Reporters at The Miami Herald and other McClatchy newspapers are withholding bylines from articles created by a new A.I. tool.

item.title: The World’s Central Banks Are Wrestling With a Gigantic Problem
item.description: Inflationary surges and economic slowdowns set off by the war in Iran, tariffs and other Trump policies have forced monetary officials to adjust, our columnist says.
item.dc:creator: Jeff Sommer
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Federal Reserve said on Wednesday that it needed to hold rates steady because “developments in the Middle East are contributing to a high level of uncertainty about the economic outlook.”

item.title: As Kentucky Derby Becomes a Bigger Business, Locals Feel Left Behind
item.description: Locals say rising ticket prices and a late start time for the Oaks race on Friday are hurting residents and restaurants. “The only thing I see is greed,” one steakhouse owner said.
item.dc:creator: Joe Drape
item.media:credit: Luke Sharrett for The New York Times
item.media:description: Pat Francis, whose family has owned Pat’s Steakhouse since 1958, is closing his doors on both Friday and Saturday to protest how Churchill Downs Inc. has transformed the Kentucky Derby business.

item.title: Obamacare Enrollment Drops Sharply as Costs Rise
item.description: Americans can’t afford the higher health insurance premiums that resulted from Congress’s refusal to extend federal tax credits.
item.dc:creator: Reed Abelson and Margot Sanger-Katz
item.category: Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act (2010)
item.media:credit: Lauren-Pruitt/The New York Times

item.title: Why So Many People Already Own Shares of Elon Musk’s SpaceX
item.description: Even before the rocket company holds a major initial public offering, many people own stock in it through so-called special purpose vehicles.
item.dc:creator: Erin Griffith and Ryan Mac
item.media:credit: Brandon Celi

item.title: Electric Cars Were a Big Deal Over a Hundred Years Ago
item.description: The very first E.V. was born in the 1830s. By the 1900s, electric carriages were dodging horse droppings in Manhattan streets.
item.dc:creator: Jim Resnick
item.media:credit: New-York Tribune, via Library of Congress
item.media:description: Electric battery-powered cars outside the Old Metropolitan Opera House on 39th Street in Manhattan in 1898. The vehicles served as early taxis in New York City.

item.title: 5 New Stockholm Bakeries
item.description: The Swedish appetite for sweets, caffeine and hanging out has led to a profusion of new convivial bakeries.
item.dc:creator: Ingrid K. Williams and Sofia Runarsdotter
item.media:credit: Sofia Runarsdotter for The New York Times

item.title: After Prison, a Financial Titan Plots an Unlikely Comeback
item.description: Bill McGlashan served time for trying to buy his son’s way into college during the Varsity Blues scandal. He hopes his new venture will restore his name — and save the planet.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.media:credit: Laura Morton for The New York Times
item.media:description: Bill McGlashan, co-founder of the start-up Oath, which uses a blend of microscopic organisms to improve yield and quality of food crops, reducing water and fertilizer use, near the company’s headquarters in San Francisco.

item.title: Oil Companies’ Huge Profits Revive Calls for Temporary Windfall Taxes
item.description: European nations imposed temporary taxes in the 2022 energy shock when Russia invaded Ukraine, but whether they can effectively help households is up for debate.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Cohen
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
item.media:description: An Exxon Mobil refinery in Texas. The United States has not been hit as hard by energy price increases as countries in Asia and Europe.

item.title: Why Countries Are Stocking Up on Gold
item.description: As risk has escalated, central banks have bought more gold to stash in reserve. A widening Middle East war could add to the urgency.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.category: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

item.title: Where in the World Is All That Gold Stored?
item.description: As central banks buy more gold, where to put all that heavy metal is an increasingly important question. Reserves must be secure and ready to trade in a crisis.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.category: Federal Reserve Bank of New York
item.media:credit: Joseph Horton for The New York Times
item.media:description: More than 60 central banks store gold at the Bank of England.

item.title: Crop Undercount Raises Questions About Reliability of U.S.D.A. Data
item.description: Corn estimates were off by 4.5 million acres last year. A lack of survey responses, not job cuts, led to the miss, the Agriculture Department said.
item.dc:creator: Kevin Draper
item.media:credit: Kathryn Gamble for The New York Times
item.media:description: A cornfield in Earlham, Iowa, last fall. The Agriculture Department botched its estimate for the size of the 2025 corn crop.

item.title: Behind Powell’s High-Stakes Decision to Stay at the Fed
item.description: Jerome H. Powell will remain a governor at the Federal Reserve after his term as chair ends, in a bid to guard against a further incursion by the Trump administration on the central bank’s independence.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jerome H. Powell on Wednesday during what is expected to be his final news conference as chair of the Federal Reserve.

item.title: U.S. Economy Grew 2 Percent in Early 2026 Even as War in Iran Began to Hit Energy Prices
item.description: Gross domestic product expanded at a 2 percent annual rate in the first three months of the year, a period including first weeks of conflict in the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: Talmon Joseph Smith
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Jon Cherry for The New York Times
item.media:description: The U.S. economy continued to prove resilient in the first three months of the year, even as energy prices soared in the early weeks of the war.

item.title: How Powell Just Complicated Trump’s Fed Plans
item.description: Traders now think the Federal Reserve will keep steady interest rates, or perhaps even increase them, amid succession intrigue at the central bank.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jay Powell has made an extraordinary decision to stay on at the central bank after his tenure as chair expires next month.

item.title: Casa, a Handyman Start-Up, Aims to Automate Home Maintenance
item.description: Casa, a company founded by former Uber executives, says it uses artificial intelligence and a stable of handymen to take care of members’ homes.
item.dc:creator: Michael J. de la Merced
item.media:credit: Kelsey McClellan for The New York Times
item.media:description: Michael Mizrahi, left, and Michael York founded Casa after working at Uber and CloudKitchens, the virtual restaurant company owned by Travis Kalanick.
